title: Concepts - Users and groups - Palantir Foundry Documentation

extractedEntities.name: User
extractedEntities.description: An authenticated individual with access to Foundry, typically defined by an external identity provider with public structured attributes

extractedEntities.name: Group
extractedEntities.description: A set of users that simplifies permission management by granting access to projects and resources collectively rather than individually

extractedEntities.name: Identity Provider
extractedEntities.description: External authentication system (e.g. Active Directory) that validates users, supplies attributes, and defines groups for Foundry access

extractedEntities.name: Provider Group
extractedEntities.description: A group synchronized from an external identity provider into Foundry, enabling centralized access management

extractedEntities.name: User Attributes
extractedEntities.description: Public structured information about a user supplied by the identity provider

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's core data platform that integrates with identity providers for user authentication and group-based access control

extractedEntities.name: Control Panel
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's administration interface for configuring authentication and organization assignment

extractedEntities.name: Project
extractedEntities.description: A Foundry resource container to which access is typically granted via groups rather than individual users

keyInsights: Users and groups are the fundamental access control primitives in Foundry, with groups being the preferred unit for granting resource access
Foundry integrates seamlessly with external identity providers allowing centralized user and group management through existing enterprise systems
Groups can be either provider-managed (synced from IdP) or Foundry-managed, enabling hybrid identity administration

title: Organization settings - Configure application access - Palantir Foundry Documentation

extractedEntities.name: Application Access
extractedEntities.description: Control Panel feature that allows administrators to control which Foundry applications are visible and accessible to specific users and groups

extractedEntities.name: Control Panel
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's administration interface where application access, roles, and organizational settings are managed

extractedEntities.name: Allowlist
extractedEntities.description: Application access configuration mode that restricts Foundry platform access to only members of specified groups

extractedEntities.name: Blocklist
extractedEntities.description: Application access configuration mode that restricts Foundry platform access for everyone except members of specified groups

extractedEntities.name: User Experience Administrator
extractedEntities.description: A Foundry role that grants the Manage Application Access workflow for configuring application visibility

extractedEntities.name: Slate
extractedEntities.description: A consumer-facing Foundry application accessible even to users with restricted platform access

extractedEntities.name: Workshop
extractedEntities.description: A consumer-facing Foundry application accessible even to users with restricted platform access

extractedEntities.name: Approvals
extractedEntities.description: Foundry governance service that tracks application access change requests with configurable approval policies

extractedEntities.name: Application Access Restriction
extractedEntities.description: Pattern of limiting visible Foundry applications to streamline experience for operational users who work within custom applications or curated analyses

keyInsights: Application access is a UX simplification feature, not a security feature; it only controls frontend visibility of applications, not actual permissions
The most restrictive configuration removes all Foundry platform access, leaving users with only consumer-facing apps like Slate and Workshop
Application access changes go through a governance workflow via Approvals, with configurable approval policies that default to self-approval
Two restriction modes exist: allowlist (only specified groups get access) and blocklist (everyone except specified groups gets access)
Beta application rollout is a common use case, giving advanced users early access while restricting availability to the broader base

title: How Palantir Enables a Secure, Rapid Software Development Environment - Palantir Blog

extractedEntities.name: Software Supply Chain Security
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's SSCS program for securing the entire software development lifecycle against advanced persistent threats and supply chain attacks

extractedEntities.name: Zero Trust
extractedEntities.description: Security architecture where all devices are assumed potentially hostile or compromised, requiring continuous verification

extractedEntities.name: Device Authentication
extractedEntities.description: Azure AD Conditional Access policies enforcing device-based authentication including managed device certificates, Intune enrollment, and platform compliance

extractedEntities.name: Conditional Access Policies
extractedEntities.description: Microsoft Azure AD policies that enforce security requirements at authentication time, including device compliance, MFA, and geographic restrictions

extractedEntities.name: Apollo
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's internally-hosted infrastructure for secure software deployment lifecycle, providing controlled build and deployment pipelines

extractedEntities.name: VPN Full Tunnel
extractedEntities.description: Network access control requiring all Palantir devices to connect through a full-tunnel VPN authenticated via SSO passwordless login

extractedEntities.name: Defense in Depth
extractedEntities.description: Security architecture layering multiple controls so compromise of one layer does not grant full access

extractedEntities.name: Advanced Persistent Threat
extractedEntities.description: Sophisticated nation-state and criminal adversaries targeting Palantir through zero-day exploitation, social engineering, and supply chain attacks

keyInsights: Palantir hosts its own infrastructure rather than relying on SaaS platforms to reduce breach likelihood from upstream vendor compromise
Zero trust and assume-breach mentalities drive Palantir's security architecture, treating all devices as potentially hostile
Device-based authentication policies enforce managed device certificates, Intune enrollment, and platform compliance at every authentication event
Palantir operates FedRAMP/IL5/IL6 accredited environments for their secure software development lifecycle
Software supply chain attacks are identified as among the greatest risks, necessitating bleeding-edge security solutions

title: Authentication - Multi-factor authentication - Palantir Foundry Documentation

extractedEntities.name: Multi-Factor Authentication
extractedEntities.description: Required Foundry authentication layer providing additional identity verification via app-based two-factor authentication or identity provider-managed MFA

extractedEntities.name: SAML 2.0
extractedEntities.description: Security Assertion Markup Language protocol used for Foundry identity provider integration and single sign-on configuration

extractedEntities.name: Two-Factor Authentication
extractedEntities.description: App-based authentication method managed within Foundry that generates codes for multi-factor verification

extractedEntities.name: Platform Administrator
extractedEntities.description: Foundry role with elevated privileges including the ability to reset user two-factor authentication methods

extractedEntities.name: Control Panel
extractedEntities.description: Foundry administration interface where MFA configuration and user management are performed

extractedEntities.name: Organization Assignment
extractedEntities.description: Foundry configuration step following MFA setup that assigns users to their organizational context

keyInsights: Multi-factor authentication is mandatory for all Foundry access, either managed through the platform or delegated to the identity provider
MFA can be managed in two modes: directly within Foundry using app-based 2FA, or through the organization's identity provider
Platform Administrators can reset user MFA methods through Control Panel's user management interface
SAML 2.0 integration is configured before MFA setup, establishing the identity provider connection first

title: Security and Privacy on Palantir: Best Practices - Bronson.AI

extractedEntities.name: Encryption at Rest
extractedEntities.description: Data protection mechanism using AES-256/128 encryption for stored data in Foundry databases

extractedEntities.name: Encryption in Transit
extractedEntities.description: Data protection mechanism using TLS 1.2+ encryption for data transmitted across networks in Foundry

extractedEntities.name: Access Controls
extractedEntities.description: Foundational security element defining user roles and permissions to ensure only authorized personnel access sensitive data

extractedEntities.name: Data Minimization
extractedEntities.description: Privacy practice of collecting only necessary data for specific purposes, supported by Foundry tooling

extractedEntities.name: Data Anonymization
extractedEntities.description: Privacy technique for removing or obfuscating personally identifiable information, with Foundry providing supporting tools

extractedEntities.name: Audit Logging
extractedEntities.description: Comprehensive tracking of user activities and data access patterns for security monitoring and compliance

extractedEntities.name: HIPAA Compliance
extractedEntities.description: Healthcare regulatory compliance framework supported by Palantir's security controls

extractedEntities.name: GDPR Compliance
extractedEntities.description: European data protection regulatory compliance supported by Palantir's privacy and security features

extractedEntities.name: ITAR Compliance
extractedEntities.description: International Traffic in Arms Regulations compliance supported by Palantir's data governance capabilities

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's data platform supporting robust encryption, access controls, audit logging, and regulatory compliance

keyInsights: Palantir supports robust encryption standards including TLS 1.2+ for transit and AES-256/128 for data at rest
Foundry provides built-in tools for data minimization and anonymization to reduce risk of sensitive data exposure
Comprehensive audit logging tracks user activities and data access patterns enabling real-time anomaly detection
Palantir platforms are designed to meet stringent compliance requirements including GDPR, HIPAA, SOC 2, and ITAR
Human error is identified as a leading cause of data breaches, making employee security training essential alongside platform controls

title: Data Connection - Listeners - Security - Palantir Foundry Documentation

extractedEntities.name: Listener
extractedEntities.description: Data Connection component that allows external systems to push data into Foundry, implementing security protocols defined by the external system rather than standard Foundry authentication

extractedEntities.name: Data Connection
extractedEntities.description: Foundry's data ingestion platform that supports listeners for external system push-based data flow

extractedEntities.name: Listener Subdomain
extractedEntities.description: Dedicated subdomain assigned to listeners for granular ingress control, governance workflows, and isolation from primary enrollment domains

extractedEntities.name: Endpoint Rotation
extractedEntities.description: Security mechanism allowing compromised listener endpoints to be migrated to new endpoints with zero downtime

extractedEntities.name: Listener Security
extractedEntities.description: Security paradigm for listeners where external system protocols define request authorization rather than standard Foundry authentication

extractedEntities.name: Markings
extractedEntities.description: Security labels that can be applied to listeners for additional access control and data governance

extractedEntities.name: Subdomain Isolation
extractedEntities.description: Pattern of isolating less secure listener endpoints from primary enrollment domains using dedicated subdomains

extractedEntities.name: Output Stream
extractedEntities.description: The data stream produced by a listener that must be secured alongside the listener itself

keyInsights: Listeners bypass standard Foundry authentication, instead implementing security protocols defined by the external systems pushing data
Dedicated subdomains provide granular ingress control and isolate less secure listener endpoints from primary Foundry enrollment domains
Endpoint rotation enables zero-downtime migration when a listener endpoint is compromised, supporting one active endpoint at a time
Data redaction on incoming listener data is best-effort only; Palantir cannot guarantee full removal of sensitive tokens from request bodies
Both the listener itself and its output stream must be secured, typically by placing both in a restricted project and applying markings

title: Introducing Conjure, Palantir's Toolchain for HTTP/JSON APIs - Hacker News

extractedEntities.name: Conjure
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's API definition toolchain for HTTP/JSON APIs that generates human-quality client and server code from declarative API definitions across multiple languages

extractedEntities.name: Conjure API Definition
extractedEntities.description: Declarative API format that constrains API design to ensure consistency across services while generating drop-in code replacements

extractedEntities.name: Conjure Backcompat Checker
extractedEntities.description: CI tool that automatically validates wire-format backward compatibility of Conjure API changes

extractedEntities.name: Semver API Versioning
extractedEntities.description: Pattern where Conjure APIs use semantic versioning with embedded minimum/maximum version constraints in microservices and frontends

extractedEntities.name: SLS Packaging
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's open-source deployment packaging tooling that manages version constraints between services

extractedEntities.name: Wire Format Compatibility
extractedEntities.description: API design constraint requiring only additive changes to requests (optional fields) and no removals from responses to maintain backward compatibility

keyInsights: Conjure was created in 2016 to replace hand-maintained JAX-RS Java services and TypeScript frontend clients with generated code from declarative API definitions
Conjure was chosen over gRPC/Protobuf because browser support for gRPC was poor at the time, and frontend-backend communication was the primary pain point
Conjure enforces strong enough constraints to ensure consistent APIs across services, unlike Swagger/OpenAPI which was too general
Deployment infrastructure enforces embedded version constraints, ensuring major API version bumps only roll out when all callers declare support
A CI backcompat checker automatically catches unintended wire-format breaking changes before they reach production

title: GitHub - palantir/java-compute-module: Java library for writing Compute Modules

extractedEntities.name: Java Compute Module SDK
extractedEntities.description: Open-source Java library for creating Compute Modules using a builder pattern with function registration, service discovery, and token management

extractedEntities.name: Compute Module
extractedEntities.description: Foundry feature enabling containerized workloads with registered functions that handle typed input/output and execute within Foundry's managed runtime

extractedEntities.name: Function
extractedEntities.description: A named unit of logic registered on a Compute Module with typed input and output classes, referenced by a unique identifier

extractedEntities.name: ComputeModule Builder
extractedEntities.description: Builder pattern API for constructing ComputeModule instances by adding functions with input/output types and identifiers via .add() method

extractedEntities.name: Context
extractedEntities.description: Runtime context object passed to Compute Module functions providing access to service discovery and authentication tokens

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's platform in which Compute Modules are deployed and executed as containerized workloads

keyInsights: Compute Modules use a builder pattern where functions are registered with typed input/output classes and unique string identifiers
The Java SDK is in incubating phase and provides ComputeModule, Context, and function registration APIs
Functions receive a Context object providing service discovery and token management alongside their typed input parameter
The SDK handles containerized execution concerns including service discovery and authentication token management transparently

title: Foundry Compute Modules: The Missing Getting Started Guide - Medium

extractedEntities.name: Compute Module
extractedEntities.description: Foundry feature for building and deploying containerized workloads, enabling developers to use any programming language for compute-intensive, long-running, or stateful processes

extractedEntities.name: Sidecar Container
extractedEntities.description: Container provided by Foundry's deployment process that polls for function invocations and passes jobs to the user's compute module container

extractedEntities.name: Job Polling
extractedEntities.description: Long-polling mechanism where the sidecar container checks for function invocations, returning 200 with job data when found or 204 to retry

extractedEntities.name: Module Auth Token
extractedEntities.description: Authentication token read from MODULE_AUTH_TOKEN environment variable, required in Module-Auth-Token header for all sidecar API calls

extractedEntities.name: Internal Query API
extractedEntities.description: Sidecar REST API at localhost:8945 with /internal-query/job for getting jobs and /internal-query/results/{jobId} for posting results

extractedEntities.name: Function
extractedEntities.description: Named computation registered in a Compute Module that processes typed queries and returns results via the sidecar container

extractedEntities.name: Compute Module Use Cases
extractedEntities.description: Job queues for optimization, reverse proxies, data caching, image processing with ImageMagick, and SSR/PDF generation with Puppeteer

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Platform where Compute Modules run as containerized workloads with integrated job routing and authentication

keyInsights: Compute Modules use a sidecar container architecture where Foundry's sidecar polls for jobs and routes them to the user's container
The sidecar exposes a localhost REST API at port 8945 for job retrieval (GET) and result posting (POST), authenticated via MODULE_AUTH_TOKEN
All jobs must have results posted back; failure to do so prevents new jobs from being routed to the worker
Compute Modules fill gaps where Foundry Functions are ill-suited: compute-intensive, long-running, or stateful processes
While giving full container control, Compute Modules trade off Foundry benefits like integrated git repositories and built-in tooling

title: Training Track - Frontend & OSDK Developer - Palantir Learn

extractedEntities.name: Ontology SDK
extractedEntities.description: SDK enabling developers to get the full power of the Ontology directly in custom frontends or backends, generated via Developer Console with support for high-scale queries and Foundry writeback

extractedEntities.name: Developer Console
extractedEntities.description: Palantir portal for creating and managing applications using Palantir APIs, generating Ontology SDKs, and optionally hosting custom React frontends

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Core Ontology entity that OSDK developers interact with for reading, querying, and writing data

extractedEntities.name: Function
extractedEntities.description: Ontology computation callable through OSDK for performing operations on Ontology data

extractedEntities.name: Interface
extractedEntities.description: Ontology abstraction accessible through OSDK that provides polymorphic access to Object Types

extractedEntities.name: Foundry as Backend
extractedEntities.description: Architecture pattern where Foundry serves as the backend for custom frontend applications, providing high-scale queries, writeback, and governance controls via OSDK

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Platform serving as the backend for OSDK-powered applications with robust query capabilities and granular governance controls

keyInsights: OSDK treats Foundry as a backend, enabling custom frontends to leverage Ontology's high-scale queries and writeback alongside granular governance controls
Developer Console is the central portal for generating OSDK, managing applications, and optionally hosting custom React frontends
The OSDK training track covers end-to-end workflows from Ontology creation through building custom React applications that read and write Ontology data
OAuth authentication is used for OSDK applications accessing data as either public or confidential clients

title: How Palantir built their GitOps Internal Developer Platform

extractedEntities.name: Apollo
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's continuous deployment platform powering GitOps-based delivery of Foundry and Gotham across hundreds of environments with thousands of daily deployments

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: One of Palantir's primary customer-facing products deployed and operated through the Apollo platform

extractedEntities.name: Gotham
extractedEntities.description: One of Palantir's primary customer-facing products deployed and operated through the Apollo platform

extractedEntities.name: GitOps
extractedEntities.description: Deployment pattern where infrastructure and application state is managed through Git repositories, chosen by Palantir for developer-friendly workflows

extractedEntities.name: Continuous Deployment
extractedEntities.description: Apollo's delivery model enabling thousands of deployments and configuration changes daily across cloud, on-premises, and edge environments

extractedEntities.name: Apollo Pull Request Scale
extractedEntities.description: Apollo adoption reached over 10,000 monthly pull requests by 2021 with linear growth since 2017

keyInsights: Apollo is Palantir's internal developer platform serving hundreds of environments with over 1,000 engineers across four-to-twelve-person teams
Palantir divided Apollo ownership between production infrastructure (~110 engineers) and internal infrastructure (<20 engineers) teams
Apollo supports three GitOps use cases: continuous deployment service management, infrastructure management, and internal developer tool management
GitOps at scale was significantly harder than expected, requiring Palantir to regroup multiple times and build a better internal developer platform
Apollo operates across hyper-scale cloud, on-premises government, and edge environments with thousands of daily deployments

title: Palantir Apollo: Real-Time Deployment in Foundry - System Soft Technologies

extractedEntities.name: Apollo
extractedEntities.description: Continuous delivery and deployment platform for Foundry that versions and promotes Ontologies, Pipelines, Workflows, Code Repositories, and UI tools across secure environments

extractedEntities.name: Object-Aware Versioning
extractedEntities.description: Apollo pattern of tracking versions at the operational layer including object types, actions, logic flows, policy rules, and UI definitions rather than just code commits

extractedEntities.name: Secure Multi-Domain Delivery
extractedEntities.description: Apollo pattern supporting deployments across disconnected networks, air-gapped servers, and cross-agency environments with cryptographically signed artifacts

extractedEntities.name: Environment-Specific Configuration
extractedEntities.description: Apollo feature for defining environment overrides including feature flags, access tiers, and compliance-specific adjustments while maintaining configuration parity

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Core Ontology entity that Apollo versions and promotes as part of deployment packages across environments

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology entity tracked by Apollo's object-aware versioning alongside object types and logic flows

extractedEntities.name: Defense Logistics Deployment
extractedEntities.description: Use case where Apollo synchronized field asset updates across four isolated networks, reducing sync errors by 40% and eliminating three manual deployment touchpoints

extractedEntities.name: Foundry
extractedEntities.description: Platform whose applications are deeply coupled with ontology models, policy enforcement layers, and object semantics that vary by domain

keyInsights: Apollo is natively aware of Foundry's object model, meaning deployments are versioned operational logic rather than just code bundles
Standard CI/CD models break in Foundry because applications are deeply coupled with ontology models, policy enforcement, and object semantics that change by domain
Object-aware versioning tracks object types, actions, logic flows, policy rules, and UI definitions as a complete deployment unit
Apollo supports deployments across air-gapped, disconnected networks with cryptographically signed artifacts and end-to-end auditability
Every deployment is traceable and reversible without affecting data integrity or downstream objects

title: Overview - Dev toolchain - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Developer Toolchain
extractedEntities.description: Palantir's suite of developer tools for building on the Ontology including APIs, SDKs, development environments, MCP integration, and compute modules

extractedEntities.name: Ontology SDK
extractedEntities.description: SDK generated from ontologies in Python, Java, and TypeScript for accessing object types, applying actions, calling functions, and running AIP Logic functions

extractedEntities.name: Developer Console
extractedEntities.description: In-platform portal providing Ontology-specific documentation, OSDK generation, application management, and VS Code workspace creation

extractedEntities.name: Palantir MCP
extractedEntities.description: Model Context Protocol implementation allowing AI IDEs and agents to autonomously build end-to-end applications in Foundry from data integration through ontology configuration

extractedEntities.name: Code Workspaces
extractedEntities.description: VS Code-based development environment integrated with Developer Console for building React applications on the Palantir platform

extractedEntities.name: Platform SDK
extractedEntities.description: Beta SDK available in Python and TypeScript for programmatic management of platform access and data, usable alongside Ontology SDKs with same platform clients

extractedEntities.name: Compute Module
extractedEntities.description: Containerized code execution unit for scaling deployments as part of the developer toolchain

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Builder
extractedEntities.description: Foundry data connection tool for building data transformation pipelines

extractedEntities.name: Code Repositories
extractedEntities.description: Foundry-hosted git repositories for code development and collaboration

extractedEntities.name: Foundry Branching
extractedEntities.description: Development feature creating safe, streamlined environments for development of end-to-end workflows

keyInsights: The developer toolchain centers on APIs and SDKs that open the Ontology to external applications, with OSDK supporting Python, Java, and TypeScript generation
Palantir MCP enables AI IDEs and agents to autonomously build end-to-end Foundry applications from data integration through ontology configuration and application development
Platform SDKs are distinct from Ontology SDKs: Platform SDKs manage platform access and data, while Ontology SDKs access ontology entities
Developer Console serves as the unified portal for OSDK generation, API documentation, application management, and VS Code workspace creation
OSDK applications use OAuth flow as public or confidential clients for data access authentication

title: Functions - Feature support by language - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Function
extractedEntities.description: Foundry computation unit available in TypeScript v1, TypeScript v2, Python, and AIP Logic with varying feature support across languages

extractedEntities.name: TypeScript v2 Functions
extractedEntities.description: Latest TypeScript function runtime running in full Node.js environment with first-class OSDK support, configurable resources up to 8 vCPUs and 5GB memory

extractedEntities.name: TypeScript v1 Functions
extractedEntities.description: Legacy TypeScript function runtime with fewer platform features than v2 but supporting functions on models and webhooks

extractedEntities.name: Python Functions
extractedEntities.description: Python function runtime supporting Pipeline Builder integration, deployed execution, external API calls, and Ontology interfaces

extractedEntities.name: AIP Logic Functions
extractedEntities.description: AI-powered function runtime supporting functions on models, bring-your-own-model, and semantic search but not interfaces or Pipeline Builder

extractedEntities.name: Interface
extractedEntities.description: Ontology abstraction accessible only in Python functions, not supported in TypeScript v1, v2, or AIP Logic

extractedEntities.name: Serverless Functions
extractedEntities.description: Function execution mode where resources are spun up on demand when invoked, supported across all language runtimes

extractedEntities.name: Deployed Functions
extractedEntities.description: Function execution mode with dedicated pre-allocated resources, supported only in TypeScript v2 and Python

extractedEntities.name: Workshop
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application from which functions can be invoked as queries across all language runtimes

extractedEntities.name: Pipeline Builder
extractedEntities.description: Foundry pipeline tool that can call Python functions but not TypeScript or AIP Logic functions

extractedEntities.name: Marketplace
extractedEntities.description: Foundry marketplace for packaging and distributing functions across all language runtimes

keyInsights: Ontology interface support in functions is exclusive to Python; TypeScript v1, v2, and AIP Logic do not support interfaces
TypeScript v2 is the recommended runtime, providing full Node.js environment, first-class OSDK support, and configurable resources up to 8 vCPUs and 5GB memory
Pipeline Builder integration is exclusive to Python functions, making Python the choice for data pipeline workflows
Functions on models and bring-your-own-model capabilities are only available in TypeScript v1 and AIP Logic, not in v2 or Python
All function runtimes support serverless execution, but deployed execution with dedicated resources is only available in TypeScript v2 and Python
Webhook support is exclusive to TypeScript v1 functions

title: Carbon - Overview - Palantir

extractedEntities.name: Carbon
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application for configuring custom platform experiences called workspaces, providing focused interfaces for operational users with streamlined navigation and curated workflows

extractedEntities.name: Workspace
extractedEntities.description: Custom Carbon-configured platform experience tailored to specific user groups with customized landing pages and configured module sets

extractedEntities.name: Module
extractedEntities.description: Parameterized application within a Carbon workspace representing a specific workflow or tool, such as a Workshop application or Quiver analysis

extractedEntities.name: Workshop
extractedEntities.description: Foundry application that can be embedded as modules within Carbon workspaces for operational workflows

extractedEntities.name: Quiver
extractedEntities.description: Foundry analytics tool that can be embedded as modules within Carbon workspaces for data analysis

extractedEntities.name: Object Type
extractedEntities.description: Core Ontology entity that Carbon workspaces interact with through Ontology-aware search and object passing between modules

extractedEntities.name: Interface
extractedEntities.description: Ontology abstraction used in Carbon for Ontology-aware search functionality across workspaces

extractedEntities.name: ObjectSet
extractedEntities.description: Collection of objects that can be passed between modules within Carbon workspaces

extractedEntities.name: Aircraft Parts Maintenance
extractedEntities.description: Example Carbon workspace use case with maintenance lists, triage actions, investigation modules, and trend analysis

extractedEntities.name: Action Type
extractedEntities.description: Ontology actions integrated into Carbon workspaces for operational workflows like triaging parts

keyInsights: Carbon creates focused experiences for operational users by selectively hiding platform complexity and restricting navigation outside the workspace
Workspaces consist of customized landing pages and configured modules that pass objects and object sets between each other
Carbon is designed for less technical users who need to carry out critical operational workflows without exposure to the full Foundry platform
Multiple workspaces can be tailored to specific user profiles with robust permissions governing access to each workspace
Modules are parameterized applications that can include Workshop apps, Foundry Actions, investigation tools, and Quiver analyses
